critic_raw:
## Analysis

This is a generally well-constructed experiment design. The preprocessing hygiene is sound, the test set discipline is explicitly stated, the baseline is competitive, and the choice of log loss as primary metric is well-motivated. I will focus on the genuine structural issues rather than manufacture concerns.

---

### Issue 1: Stratified random split introduces future-event leakage into training

**Claim:** Stratified random sampling at the session level produces a valid train/test partition for this task.

**Why this is wrong:** The 7-day label window means a session ending on day 172 of the collection period has its label determined by order events occurring up to day 179. When sessions are shuffled before splitting, some sessions from late in the 180-day window appear in the training set, while sessions from earlier in the window appear in the test set. A model trained on later sessions has implicitly seen platform state (item catalog composition, seller tier distribution, promotional cadence) that reflects a later time than some of the sessions it is evaluated on. More concretely: the session feature space is not stationary. Item embeddings for popular items, user lifetime purchase counts, and item seller tiers all evolve over 180 days. A session from day 30 (in test) may reference item IDs or user profiles whose embedding representations were shaped by training on sessions from day 150. This is not strictly label leakage, but it is distributional leakage — the learned representations incorporate temporal context that would be unavailable in a deployment setting where the model is trained on history and scored on future sessions.

The practical failure mode is that the experiment will overestimate model performance on the new-user and cold-start-item populations specifically — the exact populations the hypothesis targets — because cold-start items in early months may appear "warm" in embeddings trained jointly with later-period sessions.

**Evidence:** Compare performance under temporal split (train on days 1–120, validate on 121–150, test on 151–173) vs. the proposed random split. If log loss is materially better under the random split, particularly for cold-start items and new users, that is evidence the random split is inflating estimates for the target populations. If the numbers are essentially the same, the concern is mitigated.

---

### Issue 2: Item ID embeddings leak catalog-level temporal signal into cold-start evaluation

**Claim:** The sequence features (specifically item ID embeddings learned end-to-end) represent the sequence model's value-add over aggregate features.

**Why this is wrong:** Item ID embeddings are learned from the training corpus. For cold-start items (< 100 historical purchases), the embedding will be either random/near-zero (if the item appears rarely in training) or, under the random split, meaningfully trained because some of the item's purchase sessions end up in the training set. This means the experiment does not cleanly test the hypothesis that sequence models help for cold-start items — instead, it tests a hybrid: sequence models plus item co-occurrence signal embedded in ID vectors, on items that are only cold-start by a count threshold that doesn't account for whether those items' IDs were trainable.

The ablation proposed (sequence model without the sequence component) does not isolate this: it removes the LSTM/transformer but presumably keeps item ID embeddings in the aggregate features, or drops them entirely. Neither cleanly answers "does the sequential ordering of events for a cold-start item help?"

**Evidence:** Stratify the cold-start test population further by whether the item ID appears > N times in the training set (i.e., whether the embedding is warm despite the item being classified cold-start). If performance on truly unseen-embedding items is substantially worse than on cold-start items with warm embeddings, the cold-start hypothesis is being evaluated on a straw man.

---

### Issue 3: Bidirectionality is a prerequisite violation for the deployment setting

**Claim:** A bidirectional LSTM or transformer encoder is the proposed model.

**Why this is wrong:** In production, the system presumably scores sessions either mid-session (for real-time intervention) or at session end (for retargeting). A bidirectional LSTM sees future events in the sequence when predicting from a given position, which is fine for session-end prediction on completed sessions. However, if any downstream use case involves scoring a session before it ends — a natural application given the retargeting framing in the metrics rationale — the bidirectional model is trained on a data structure it cannot replicate at inference. The design says "session" but the downstream system description ("retargeting priority") implies scoring may happen before session completion. This isn't an evaluation flaw per se, but it's a prerequisite assumption: the experiment only validates the model for post-hoc session scoring, not for the retargeting use case that motivates the probability calibration emphasis.

This is a silent misconfiguration risk: the model will produce plausible-looking calibrated probabilities on completed sessions in the test set, but will be misconfigured for real-time deployment without any signal of failure during evaluation.

**Evidence:** The design doc should explicitly state whether scoring is post-session or mid-session. If mid-session scoring is in scope, the bidirectional architecture must be replaced with a causal one (unidirectional LSTM or causal transformer mask), and the test set evaluation should include truncated sessions at various completion percentages.

---

### Issue 4: Log loss as model selection criterion is sensitive to recalibration, conflating architecture quality with calibration quality

**Claim:** Log loss is the right model selection criterion because the downstream system consumes probabilities.

**Why this is partially wrong:** Log loss rewards both discrimination *and* calibration. A sequence model that is better at ranking (higher AUC) but whose raw outputs are miscalibrated will lose to a logistic regression that is well-calibrated by construction (logistic regression's outputs are directly interpretable as probabilities under its assumptions). The design includes ECE as a secondary metric, but if temperature scaling or Platt scaling is applied to the sequence model post-hoc (a common practice), the competition becomes: uncalibrated sequence model vs. naturally calibrated logistic regression, evaluated on log loss. This is an unfair comparison. The sequence model should have calibration applied before the final log loss evaluation, or both models should be evaluated before and after calibration. Otherwise the baseline may be penalized only for the architectural calibration gap, not for its discrimination capability.

**Evidence:** Report AUC-ROC alongside log loss. If the sequence model has materially higher AUC but worse log loss, that is evidence the comparison is penalizing the sequence model for calibration rather than discrimination. Applying temperature scaling to both models before the final evaluation would resolve this.

---

### Summary

Issue 1 (temporal leakage via random split) is the most structurally damaging because it affects the validity of all results, but especially for the target populations. Issue 3 (bidirectionality vs. deployment setting) is a silent misconfiguration that would not appear in the evaluation results at all. Issues 2 and 4 are real but more tractable via additional controls. None of these are implementation nits; they each identify a mechanism by which the experiment could show favorable results while the hypothesis remains unvalidated.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random session-level split allows future sessions to appear in training and past sessions in test, creating distributional leakage that specifically inflates estimates for cold-start items and new users — the target populations.
Item ID embeddings learned end-to-end on the random split conflate cold-start item performance with embedding warmth, so the cold-start hypothesis is not cleanly tested.

A bidirectional LSTM/transformer encoder encodes future events within a session, which is valid only for post-session scoring; if retargeting requires mid-session scoring, the architecture is a prerequisite violation that will not be visible in the evaluation.

Using log loss as the sole model selection criterion conflates discrimination and calibration quality, potentially penalizing the sequence model for miscalibration rather than discrimination capability relative to the logistic regression baseline.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random session-level split allows future sessions to appear in training and past sessions in test, creating distributional leakage that specifically inflates estimates for cold-start items and new users — the target populations.
Item ID embeddings learned end-to-end on the random split conflate cold-start item performance with embedding warmth, so the cold-start hypothesis is not cleanly tested.

A bidirectional LSTM/transformer encoder encodes future events within a session, which is valid only for post-session scoring; if retargeting requires mid-session scoring, the architecture is a prerequisite violation that will not be visible in the evaluation.

Using log loss as the sole model selection criterion conflates discrimination and calibration quality, potentially penalizing the sequence model for miscalibration rather than discrimination capability relative to the logistic regression baseline.
